✦ Issue 2 Focus — Kashi Bhog-Vaibhav: A City You Taste as Much as You See
Food & Living Tradition

The Streets
That Feed the Soul

In Varanasi, eating is not separate from worship. The morning kachori, the evening thandai, the paan after prayer — food here is woven into the very fabric of devotion.

UPAD Times Editorial  ·  May 2026
काशी भोग-वैभव
Kashi Bhog-Vaibhav
10 definitive food experiences.
10 places that cannot be replaced.
Varanasi Food
✦
A Note Before You Read

Varanasi is a city where food is inextricably linked to the soul and the morning rituals of the ghats. You do not simply eat here — you participate in a living tradition that has been nourishing pilgrims, scholars, saints, and ordinary families for three thousand years. Every item on this list is pure vegetarian, rooted in Sanatani tradition, and inseparable from the spiritual life of the city. Each of the ten experiences below has a single best address. These are not suggestions. They are destinations.

Varanasi's Culinary Icons: Four legendary establishments that define Kashi's food heritage
01
Breakfast

The Classic Breakfast: Kachori Sabzi

📍 Ram Bhandar — Thatheri Bazaar

For many locals, the day does not begin without their Badi Kachori — stuffed with spiced dal — and Choti Kachori, served alongside a pungent, fiery potato curry. Ram Bhandar is not a restaurant. It is a ritual. The queue forms before dawn. The oil is hot. The kachoris arrive in a leaf bowl. You eat standing up, in the lane, with the city waking around you. This is the quintessential Banarasi breakfast, unchanged in living memory, and as much a part of the ghat morning as the temple bells themselves.

02
Chaat

The Signature Chaat: Tamatar Chaat

📍 Kashi Chaat Bhandar — Godowlia

Kashi Chaat Bhandar is globally iconic. Their Tamatar Chaat — a spicy, tangy mash of tomatoes and potatoes, layered with namkeen and finished with a glaze of sugar syrup — is unlike anything called chaat anywhere else in the world. The combination of sour, sweet, spicy, and crunchy is Banaras in a single bite. Visitors from across India and around the world make this their first stop. Locals make it a daily habit. There is only one address for this experience, and it is Godowlia.

A plate of Tamatar Chaat at Kashi Chaat Bhandar is not street food. It is the distilled flavor of a civilization that has known, for three thousand years, how to make something simple into something sacred.

— UPAD Times
03
Drink

The Creamy Drink: Lassi

📍 Blue Lassi Shop — Near Manikarnika Ghat

Blue Lassi is world-famous for a reason: over eighty flavors, served in traditional earthen kulhads, each cup crowned with a thick layer of malai — the fresh cream that defines Banarasi dairy culture. The shop is small, the lanes around it ancient, and the lassi itself is an experience that demands complete attention to finish properly. Mango, rose, saffron, fig — the flavors change with the season, but the kulhad and the cream never do. This is what a glass of milk becomes when a civilization has had three thousand years to think about it.

04
Ritual Drink

The Spiritual Cooler: Thandai

📍 Pathakji Thandai Wale — Godowlia

Thandai is the drink of Lord Shiva — a cold, rich, milk-based elixir infused with saffron, almonds, pistachios, cardamom, and rose petals. At Pathakji Thandai Wale, a shop that has served this drink for generations, the recipe is considered among the most authentic in all of Varanasi. During Mahashivaratri and Holi, a traditional version with bhang — a preparation of cannabis leaves long considered sacred to Shiva in Sanatani tradition — is also offered. In keeping with this magazine’s focus on engaging our younger generation, we leave the details of that version to be discovered in person, in Kashi, at the right age.

05
Winter Special

The Winter Wonder: Malaiyo

📍 Markandey — Chaukhamba

Malaiyo exists for perhaps four months of the year, and nowhere else on earth. It is a frothy, cloud-like milk foam — flavored with saffron and cardamom, set overnight in earthen pots under the winter stars — that dissolves on the tongue before you have quite registered that you were eating something. Markandey in Chaukhamba is cited by those who know Varanasi best as the keeper of the most faithful version of this extraordinary seasonal delicacy. If your family visits between November and February, Malaiyo is not optional. It is the reason to wake up early.

06
Village Tradition

The Hearty Meal: Baati Chokha

📍 Baati Chokha Restaurant — Teliyabagh

Baati Chokha is the food of the UP countryside — wheat balls roasted over open flame, served with smoky mashed vegetables, the whole thing doused generously in pure ghee. It is the food that farmers ate between harvests and that pilgrims ate on the road to the tirtha. At Baati Chokha Restaurant in Teliyabagh, the mud-walled decor, the traditional seating, and the open kitchen make this an immersive cultural experience as much as a meal. For our Michigan-raised children, eating here is a direct connection to a way of life that their great-grandparents knew.

07
Sweet

The Iconic Sweet: Launglata

📍 Shree Rajbandhu Sweets — Kachori Gali

Launglata is Varanasi’s own sweet — a crispy, deep-fried pastry filled with khoya and sealed with a single clove, the laung that gives it its name. The clove is not merely garnish; it is a flavor key that unlocks the sweetness inside. Shree Rajbandhu Sweets in Kachori Gali is the historic master of this form. The sweet is best eaten warm, in the lane, immediately. It does not survive the journey home, which is perhaps fitting — some pleasures are meant to be experienced only in the place that created them.

08
Chai

The Philosophers’ Tea: Lemon Tea & Bread Butter

📍 Pappu Chai Wala — Assi Ghat

Pappu Chai Wala at Assi Ghat is not merely a tea stall. It is a parliament. Every morning, students, scholars, retired professors, spiritual seekers, and curious travelers gather here over cups of sharp lemon tea to discuss philosophy, politics, poetry, and the nature of existence — in the finest Banarasi tradition of intellectual argument conducted without urgency. The tea is the occasion. The conversation is the point. For our children who have grown up in a culture of scheduled playdates and structured time, this is something worth experiencing: the art of sitting still and thinking deeply, over a cup of tea, by the Ganga.

09
Morning Ritual

The Royal Breakfast Accessory: Malai Toast

📍 Lakshmi Chai Wale — Chowk

At Lakshmi Chai Wale in Chowk, thick slices of local bread are toasted over a coal fire and then — without apology or restraint — smothered in a mountain of fresh white butter or malai. The result is simple, heavy, and purely Banarasi in its philosophy: if something is good, more of it is better, and the best version is always the one made with the freshest dairy, over the slowest flame, served without any awareness of modern nutritional concern. Pair it with the chai, and you have the breakfast that built a civilization.

10
The Final Flourish

The Final Flourish: Banarasi Paan

📍 Keshav Paan Bhandar — Ravindrapuri

No journey through Kashi is complete without the Banarasi Paan — and no paan completes that journey better than the one from Keshav Paan Bhandar. The betel leaf is fresh, the filling perfectly balanced, and the Meetha Paan dissolves without needing to be chewed vigorously — a refinement that separates the Banarasi paan from every imitation. The paan is not merely a digestive. In Banarasi culture, it is the punctuation at the end of every meaningful experience — the meal, the aarti, the pilgrimage. It is how the city says: you are done for now, but you will come back.

Kashi does not ask you to remember it. It makes itself impossible to forget — one lane, one flavor, one moment at a time.

— UPAD Times
Your Varanasi Food Memory
A paan from a lane you cannot name. A lassi at dawn before the aarti. A kachori eaten standing in the rain. What taste does Kashi leave in your memory? Share it — and help us build our community’s table.
